name: Garry D. Peterson
title: Professor of Resilience in Social-Ecological Systems
intro: I study how people and nature shape one another, especially when social-ecological systems change in surprising ways. My work combines resilience thinking, comparative analysis, participatory scenarios, and open research resources to help people understand change and explore more desirable futures.
bio: Garry Peterson is a professor in environmental sciences with emphasis on resilience and social-ecological systems at the Stockholm Resilience Centre, Stockholm University. He directs Mistra Finance to Revive Biodiversity (FinBio), an international research programme connecting biodiversity science, finance, and practical action toward a nature-positive economy.

His research integrates three themes: connections between people and nature, the dynamics of resilience, and how thinking about the future can help people and organizations navigate surprising social-ecological change. He combines quantitative analysis, systems modelling, qualitative analysis, and participatory research.

Peterson has an interdisciplinary background in sustainability. He earned a bachelor's degree in Systems Design Engineering from the University of Waterloo and completed his PhD, Contagious disturbance and ecological resilience, in Zoology at the University of Florida in 1999. He has worked at the National Center for Ecological Analysis and Synthesis, the University of Wisconsin-Madison's Center for Limnology, and McGill University.

For more than twenty years, he has contributed to international science-policy assessments, including the Millennium Ecosystem Assessment, the Arctic Resilience Assessment, and IPBES work on scenarios and models. He is co-chair of the Resilience Alliance and serves on the transition team establishing the Society for Social-Ecological Systems.

research_areas.name: Resilience & regime shifts
research_areas.summary: Understanding how ecosystems and social-ecological systems persist, reorganize, or cross thresholds under disturbance and change.

research_areas.name: Ecosystem services
research_areas.summary: Studying bundles, trade-offs, scales, and governance of the benefits people receive from ecosystems.

research_areas.name: Scenarios & futures
research_areas.summary: Developing participatory and analytical approaches to imagine, compare, and navigate alternative social-ecological futures.

research_areas.name: Biodiversity finance
research_areas.summary: Connecting biodiversity science with financial decision-making, risk, reporting, and pathways toward a nature-positive economy.

research_areas.name: Social-ecological systems
research_areas.summary: Integrating social and natural science to understand cross-scale interactions, transformation, governance, and sustainability.

current_roles.organization: Transformative Futures research theme, Stockholm Resilience Centre

projects.name: Mistra Finance to Revive Biodiversity

audiences.label: For researchers and students
audiences.title: Trace the ideas and evidence
audiences.summary: Move from core concepts to publications, talks, and reusable comparative data on resilience, scenarios, and social-ecological change.

audiences.label: For policy, finance, and practice
audiences.title: Connect science to decisions
audiences.summary: Find work on biodiversity-related risk, institutional change, scenario planning, and practical pathways toward resilient, nature-positive economies.

audiences.label: For futures practitioners and educators
audiences.title: Use cases to imagine alternatives
audiences.summary: Draw on open scenario collections, sustainability initiatives, and teaching resources to help groups explore diverse and desirable futures.

lenses.label: Resilience
lenses.title: How change travels through a social-ecological system
lenses.summary: Resilience research follows feedbacks across ecological processes, lived experience, knowledge, and institutions to understand persistence, thresholds, and reorganization.
lenses.question: Where can intervention maintain valued functions, create room to adapt, or prevent harmful cascades?

biosphere.title: Biosphere
biosphere.body: Species, ecological functions, disturbances, and feedbacks create the changing biophysical conditions within which societies live.

people.title: People
people.body: Livelihoods, values, health, and collective memory shape how change is experienced and which forms of resilience are considered desirable.

knowledge.title: Knowledge
knowledge.body: Monitoring, models, local experience, and plural knowledge systems help reveal feedbacks while also making uncertainty visible.

institutions.title: Institutions
institutions.body: Governance can buffer disturbance, reinforce vulnerability, or create the flexibility needed for adaptation and transformation.

finance.title: Finance
finance.body: Capital can amplify extraction or support restoration, but financial responses need to account for cross-scale ecological feedbacks.

futures.title: Futures
futures.body: Scenarios expose assumptions about stability and surprise, helping groups test how today’s choices may alter future resilience.

lenses.label: Futures
lenses.title: How imagined futures reshape choices in the present
lenses.summary: Futures work connects plural values, grounded examples, and social-ecological knowledge so that communities and organizations can compare pathways rather than inherit a single forecast.
lenses.question: Which futures become thinkable, for whom, and how do scenarios change present-day relationships and decisions?

biosphere.title: Biosphere
biosphere.body: Scenarios consider how ecosystems, biodiversity, and Earth-system feedbacks might respond under different pathways.

people.title: People
people.body: Desirable futures differ across communities. Participatory processes make values, conflicts, hopes, and agency part of the analysis.

knowledge.title: Knowledge
knowledge.body: Models, narratives, local experience, and creative methods can be combined to explore uncertainty without pretending to predict it away.

institutions.title: Institutions
institutions.body: Rules and organizations stabilize some pathways and exclude others; scenarios help reveal where institutional change may open alternatives.

finance.title: Finance
finance.body: Investment choices shape which infrastructures and activities expand, making finance a powerful mechanism for locking in or redirecting futures.

futures.title: Futures
futures.body: Futures are not forecasts. They are structured ways to explore alternatives, surprises, trade-offs, and the consequences of different values.

lenses.label: Biodiversity finance
lenses.title: How financial systems shape biodiversity—and are shaped by it
lenses.summary: Biodiversity finance research links ecological dependencies and impacts with financial functions, regulation, reporting, and competing ideas of what a nature-positive future should value.
lenses.question: How can finance support ecological resilience and plural nature values without reducing living systems to a single price or metric?

biosphere.title: Biosphere
biosphere.body: Economic activity depends on living systems while also altering them; these two-way relationships are uneven, dynamic, and often poorly represented.

people.title: People
people.body: Financial and ecological change create different risks and opportunities across places and groups, raising questions of participation, equity, and legitimacy.

knowledge.title: Knowledge
knowledge.body: Ecological monitoring, scenarios, accounting, and risk analysis each illuminate part of the system; no single measure captures biodiversity’s plurality.

institutions.title: Institutions
institutions.body: Disclosure standards, mandates, regulation, and governance influence what financial actors notice, value, and are able or required to change.

finance.title: Finance
finance.body: Banks, investors, insurers, and public finance distribute resources and risk, influencing which economic activities grow and which decline.

futures.title: Futures
futures.body: Exploratory scenarios can test how financial functions might operate under different relationships with nature and different conceptions of value.

research_stories.title: When biodiversity loss becomes a financial question
research_stories.lead: Financial decisions shape economies that depend on living systems, yet ecological dependencies, impacts, and plural values are still only partially represented in financial practice.
research_stories.evidence: FinBio brings ecology, finance, law, accounting, futures, and decision science together to develop scenarios, evidence, and practical approaches for nature-positive financial systems.
research_stories.use: For researchers, financial institutions, regulators, and standard-setters working on biodiversity-related dependencies, impacts, future risks, and institutional change.
research_stories.open_question: How can financial rules and incentives change without reducing biodiversity to one metric, privileging only instrumental values, or shifting harms elsewhere?

research_stories.title: Using existing initiatives to imagine diverse desirable futures
research_stories.lead: Sustainability narratives often begin with crisis. The Seeds initiative begins with real initiatives already creating different relationships between people and nature, then asks what they could become.
research_stories.evidence: The open database supports comparison among sustainability initiatives and provides grounded material for scenario processes that combine empirical examples with plural visions of the future.
research_stories.use: For educators, communities, scenario practitioners, and researchers seeking constructive, evidence-based starting points for conversations about transformation.
research_stories.open_question: Which seeds can grow, combine, or transform at larger scales—and which may lose their value when removed from local histories and relationships?

research_stories.title: Following abrupt change across systems and scales
research_stories.lead: Abrupt ecological changes rarely remain isolated. Drivers, feedbacks, and responses can connect ecosystems, economies, and institutions, producing persistent and sometimes cascading change.
research_stories.evidence: The Regime Shifts Database organizes types and examples of persistent social-ecological change. Comparative research on cascading regime shifts highlights why cross-scale interactions matter for risk and resilience.
research_stories.use: For scientists, managers, and decision-makers who need to compare system changes, identify recurring drivers, and consider where monitoring or intervention may be most useful.
research_stories.open_question: Which combinations of feedbacks make cascades more likely, and where can action interrupt a cascade without creating new vulnerabilities?

research_uses.title: Explore and compare cases
research_uses.description: Use open databases to examine regime shifts, sustainability initiatives, and place-based scenario processes across regions and systems.

research_uses.title: Build and facilitate scenarios
research_uses.description: Draw on cases, futures methods, and examples of plural pathways when designing participatory scenario and foresight processes.

research_uses.title: Teach social-ecological systems
research_uses.description: Combine recorded talks with open teaching resources on methods for researching and working with social-ecological systems.

research_uses.title: Connect biodiversity and finance
research_uses.description: Follow research on nature-related financial systems, scenarios, disclosure, accounting, and pathways toward a resilient nature-positive economy.

research_uses.title: Find and cite the research
research_uses.description: Search the publication catalogue or follow verified researcher profiles for identifiers, citations, and newly indexed work.

research_uses.title: Collaborate or invite a contribution
research_uses.description: Get in touch about research collaboration, science-policy work, scenario processes, talks, or use of the open databases.

intellectual_questions.title: How are people and nature connected?
intellectual_questions.description: Biodiversity, ecosystem services, landscapes, livelihoods, institutions, and financial dependencies reveal how ecological and social systems jointly shape human well-being.

intellectual_questions.title: How and why do social-ecological systems change?
intellectual_questions.description: Feedbacks, thresholds, disturbance, resilience, and interactions across scales help explain persistence, surprise, and abrupt reorganization.

intellectual_questions.title: How can people navigate and shape uncertain futures?
intellectual_questions.description: Scenarios, plural values, collective action, governance, and institutional change can help people explore alternatives and build more desirable pathways.

current_focus.title: Finance to Revive Biodiversity
current_focus.description: FinBio connects ecology, finance, law, accounting, and futures research to help financial systems recognize their dependencies and impacts on biodiversity—and support a resilient, nature-positive economy.

current_focus.title: Transformative Futures
current_focus.description: Research on how scenarios, plural values, imagination, and collective action can help people navigate uncertainty and create more diverse, desirable social-ecological futures.
